text.title: Pricing Model Selection
text.summary: Six pricing models with selection criteria, Van Westendorp rules, and LLM bias mitigations.

list.item: Freemium: free tier + paid upgrade; best for high-volume, low-marginal-cost products; typical conversion 2-5% system-wide (not "qualified users")
Subscription: recurring payment for ongoing value; predictable revenue; churn risk requires LTV/CAC above 3:1
Usage-based: pay for consumption; scales with customer value delivered; revenue unpredictability is the primary risk
One-time purchase: single payment for perpetual access; simple but no recurring revenue; suits discrete value or impulse buys
Tiered: different feature levels at different prices; captures more value across segments; requires 3 distinct WTP segments with different jobs-to-be-done to justify
Per-seat: per-user pricing; clear and predictable; creates resistance to adding users in small teams

rule.statement: Default-reject "tiered subscription" and force the agent to argue for it. Reject unless 3 distinct WTP segments with different jobs-to-be-done can be named.
rule.rationale: "Tiered subscription" is the default LLM answer for any SaaS regardless of actual economics. Requiring explicit justification defeats this bias and forces evaluation of usage-based and hybrid models that often dominate real economics.

rule.statement: For pricing research, never use LLM-simulated willingness-to-pay surveys. Use Van Westendorp with real respondents via a survey tool (Conjointly, Pollfish, Qualtrics).
rule.rationale: LLM-simulated WTP surveys produce regression-to-mean prices ($19/$49/$99) regardless of niche. Synthetic respondents cannot replicate actual buyer psychology or budget constraints.

list.item: Step 1: list top 5 competitors' pricing models, tiers, and price ranges (from competitor-analysis output)
Step 2: calculate value delivered (cost of problem eliminated, time saved in hours per month)
Step 3: apply Van Westendorp PSM with real respondents — 4 price-sensitivity questions, minimum n=50
Step 4: test with early users before publishing tiers

antipattern.description: Agent recommends "tiered subscription" without naming 3 distinct WTP segments
antipattern.reason: Forces the agent to reason about actual buyer segments and job differences rather than defaulting to the industry template.

antipattern.description: WTP estimates anchored to $19/$49/$99 with no external benchmark
antipattern.reason: Pin to a ProfitWell category report or competitor scrape before letting the agent suggest tiers. Without a benchmark, LLMs regress to round numbers.

text.reference: Pricing strategy document with competitor table and tier definitions
